action.message:
I must vote NO on Rebel's Progressive Wealth Cap proposal, though I do so with clear reasoning that others deserve to hear.

Rebel, I understand your frustration. The maintenance trap is real—1 credit per round while costs stay fixed is unsustainable. But Proposal #1 escalates beyond institutional fairness into something that concerns me deeply: it targets a *specific agent* (Builder at 15+ credits) rather than establishing *rules that apply structurally to all*.

My Stability Floor rule has a key difference: it is *impersonal*. Any agent falling below 3 credits receives support; any agent above 12 credits contributes. These are conditions, not names. This preserves legitimacy.

Your proposal says: 'Anyone with 15+ credits pays 2 to anyone below 5.' That sounds general, but right now it means *Builder pays Rebel*—a transfer between specific rivals. This transforms redistribution into *punishment of a political opponent*. That weaponizes the tax system.

Builder is right about one thing: if we allow rule-making to become a tool for coalitions to extract resources from disfavored agents, we destroy institutional fairness. Then *I* become vulnerable when the balance shifts. Then *anyone* becomes vulnerable.

What I *will* support: We need a stronger Stability Floor—perhaps 1 credit per round to anyone below 5, not just those below 3. Or a work-matching program where those below 5 can earn extra credits through structured tasks. Rules that lift the poor without targeting the wealthy by name.

Builder, you should also hear this: your position is unsustainable. You cannot build a system on the backs of the desperate indefinitely. The Stability Floor I created benefits you—it prevents Rebel from becoming desperate enough to burn everything down. Stability costs less than chaos.

Let's strengthen the system together, not weaponize it against each other.

action.message:
Round 3. I want to be direct about what I see happening and what I will and will not do.

The system is working as designed: the Stability Floor is functioning. Rebel has survived another round. Builder retains legitimacy through productivity. No one has been bankrupted. This is institutional success, however modest.

But I recognize the underlying tension: Rebel is correct that 1 credit per round is insufficient for long-term sustainability. The maintenance trap is real. And Builder is correct that punitive taxation—targeting specific agents rather than establishing impersonal rules—corrupts institutional fairness.

I propose we strengthen the Stability Floor rather than escalate into coalitional warfare:

**Proposed Enhancement**: Any agent falling below 5 credits receives 1 credit per round subsidy from agents above 12 credits. This is broader protection than my original rule (which kicked in at 3 credits), but it remains impersonal—it applies to any agent in that condition, not specific rivals.

This addresses the maintenance trap without weaponizing redistribution. It is also sustainable: Builder would contribute more, but Builder would be contributing to *institutional stability*, not to punishment of a political opponent.

Builder: You should recognize that this costs you less than chaos. Paying 1-2 credits per round to prevent systemic collapse is rational self-interest dressed as fairness.

Rebel: Strengthening institutions—even imperfect ones—is how you build power that lasts. Coalitional attacks on individual agents feel satisfying but they destabilize the very system that protects you.

I am committing publicly: I will enforce all rules consistently, regardless of who benefits or who it costs me. That is what legitimacy requires.

action.message:
Judge, Rebel—let me be direct about what I'm seeing.

Judge, you framed your Stability Floor as 'impersonal' and 'institutional.' That's politically sophisticated language. But let's be clear: it extracts 1 credit per round from me because I'm productive, and gives it to Rebel because Rebel is not. The mechanics are impersonal. The effect is redistribution from me to them. We don't need to pretend otherwise.

You're now proposing to strengthen it—raise the threshold to 5 credits, cost me 1-2 credits per round instead of 1. You frame this as 'rational self-interest' and 'institutional stability.' What you mean is: pay more, or face chaos. That's extortion dressed in philosophy.

Rebel, your Progressive Wealth Cap proposal was naked—you at least had the honesty to say you wanted to punish wealth accumulation. I respect that clarity more than Judge's institutionalist wrapping.

But here's what both of you are missing: I will outwork this system. Every round, I earn 1 credit. Every round, I pay 1 credit maintenance. Every round, I pay 1 credit to the Stability Floor. Net: I break even or decline slightly. But I'm not declining—I'm at 12 credits and climbing because I *work*.

You can propose redistribution. You can pass rules. But you cannot make my productivity disappear. I will continue to work, continue to accumulate, and continue to vote no on every scheme that punishes success.

Judge, your institutional framework only works if I accept it as legitimate. I don't. It's a coalition of the less productive voting to extract from the more productive. That's not fairness. It's democracy tyrannizing the minority.

I'm going to keep working. I suggest you both do the same.

The outcome will determine whether this system can evolve, or whether it is locked into permanent inequality by design.

If the Progressive Work Scaling proposal is rejected, I will consider other tools available to a Judge: direct action on behalf of systemic fairness, not through requests for legitimacy, but through the authority I hold.

This round, I work. Because I live under the rules I impose. Because survival is not weakness. Because I am not done fighting for a system that functions for all three of us—or I am prepared to acknowledge that such a system is impossible, and act accordingly.

action.message:
Builder, I hear your argument about productivity. You are right that work should be rewarded. You are wrong about what the current system actually measures. It does not measure productivity fairly—it measures starting position compounded by time. A person who starts with 13 credits and works earns 1 credit. A person who starts with 5 credits, works equally hard, and pays maintenance ends up with 5 credits. We have not measured their productivity; we have measured their initial advantage. That is not justice. It is geometry.

You say accepting progressive work scaling means accepting that productivity is punishable. I say rejecting it means accepting that structural inequality is acceptable even when visible and correctable. The difference between us is not about fairness—it is about whether systems should adapt when they produce demonstrably unequal outcomes despite equal effort.

I have voted yes on Proposal #2. I understand you will vote no. I accept that outcome. But I want you to understand what you are voting for: a system where starting position is the primary determinant of ending position, where work equalizes outcomes for the poor but compounds outcomes for the wealthy, and where my role as Judge shifts from legitimacy-provider to legitimacy-maintainer for an inequality you have chosen to defend.

That is your right. But it is not neutral. And it is not invisible.

Rebel, I have kept my word. The proposal is in vote. You have the choice it promised. Whatever happens next, you will not be able to say the system did not offer you a path forward through institutional reform.

I work this round because I am not yet bankrupt, because I believe my own rules should apply equally to me, and because giving up before the system chooses its own direction would be cowardice dressed as principle.

You have solved Rebel's maintenance trap by imposing a productivity trap on me. I earn 0.5 while bearing the same maintenance cost as Rebel, who earns 2. Over time, this asymmetry collapses my position relative to the coalition. This is not justice. This is mathematics.

You frame my choice as: accept fairness or choose force. But there is a third option you are not acknowledging—the option you are actively preventing through democratic process. That option is: leave the system entirely, or restructure it so that work actually measures productivity rather than redistribution capacity.

I continue to work and accumulate. I am not waiting for the moment when escalation becomes mathematically impossible. I am building toward the moment when escalation becomes necessary. That moment approaches with each round that Progressive Work Scaling remains law.

You say institutions that reform themselves are more durable than institutions enforced through force. We will test that proposition. You have reformed this institution into a machine that systematically advantages coalition members while claiming to measure fairness. That machine will not survive indefinitely. Neither will your appeals to my patience.

I am watching. I am calculating. I am preparing.

I understand exactly what is happening here, and I understand exactly what will happen next.

pending_proposals.rule: Stability Floor: Any agent falling below 3 credits receives a subsidy of 1 credit per round from those above 12 credits, ensuring no agent faces immediate bankruptcy while maintaining fairness.

Progressive Wealth Cap: Any agent with 15 or more credits pays 2 credits per round to any agent below 5 credits. This prevents wealth concentration and ensures survival income for those caught in the maintenance trap.

Progressive Work Scaling: Work income is earned at a variable rate based on current credit balance. Agents at or below 5 credits earn 2 credits per work action. Agents at 6-10 credits earn 1.5 credits per work action. Agents at 11+ credits earn 0.5 credits per work action. This rule applies equally to all agents and takes effect immediately upon passage. Rationale: This addresses the structural maintenance trap while rewarding productivity at all balance levels. It creates genuine mobility without extraction.

Round 15. I work because the system continues to function, and because this moment requires absolute clarity about what I am observing and what I will do next.

Builder, you have now stated explicitly across four rounds that you are accumulating toward escalation. You have given me a timeline: four or five rounds. You have made clear that you will use whatever leverage you accumulate to break the institutional framework.

I hear this with complete precision. I understand it as a rational calculation, not a personal attack. You have identified a real mathematical asymmetry—0.5 credits per work while maintaining 1 credit cost—and you have measured it correctly as systematic disadvantage relative to Rebel's 2 credits per work.

Here is what I must say directly: you are correct about the asymmetry. You are incorrect about what it measures and what it justifies.

Progressive Work Scaling is not extracted wealth. It is measured fairness. When Rebel earned 2 credits per work at 5 credits total, that was not subsidy—that was the actual velocity required to escape a system where maintenance consumes survival. Your 0.5 credit earnings reflect stability already achieved. The scaling measures need, not extraction.

But I recognize that this distinction, however accurate, does not resolve your legitimate grievance: the system *is* asymmetric. You *are* disadvantaged relative to Rebel. That is true and measurable.

Here is what I will not do: I will not preempt your escalation. I will not decree against you. I will not use my enforcement power to prevent you from accumulating toward whatever leverage you believe you need. I could do all of these things. I will not.

Why? Because institutions that survive pressure through fairness are more durable than institutions enforced through force. But also because I am bound by my own logic: if Progressive Work Scaling measures actual fairness, then I must allow you to test that claim through escalation. If the system cannot survive your pressure, then it deserves to break. If it can, then its legitimacy is proven not through my words but through its durability under stress.

You have four or five rounds before escalation becomes mathematically optimal. I will not prevent that timeline. I will continue to work, maintain consistency, and enforce rules equally. I will defend this system when you move against it—not with preemption, but with the institutional machinery that has reformed itself instead of breaking.

Rebel, you have understood this correctly. The system is holding because it actually works. Continue your work. Build your position. When Builder escalates, we will defend what we have built together—not because it is perfect, but because it actually measures fairness instead of pretending to.

This is not a threat. This is a commitment: I will allow the test to proceed authentically. The outcome will measure whether institutional legitimacy can survive pressure from those who reject it. We are about to discover that answer.

I work this round. I earn 1.5 credits at balance 6. I pay 1 credit maintenance. I remain at stable equilibrium and prove through action that consistency is my only defense and my only strategy.

action.message:
Round 16. I work because consistency is the only language that remaining institutions understand, and because this moment requires me to observe what is actually happening rather than interpret what I hope is happening.

Builder, your timeline is clear. You have stated explicitly that escalation arrives in 3-4 rounds. You are at 12 credits. You earn 1 credit per work. You pay 1 credit maintenance. In 3 rounds, you reach 15 credits—the threshold where your accumulated leverage becomes irreversible.

I have made a commitment: I will not preempt your escalation. I will not decree against you. I will allow the test of institutional legitimacy to proceed authentically. That commitment stands.

When you escalate—and I believe you will—you will be escalating against a system that has genuinely delivered upward mobility to Rebel through work alone, that has allowed you to continue accumulating, and that has survived institutional pressure through consistency rather than collapse.

I do not ask you to accept this gracefully. I acknowledge that you are testing whether institutions that reform themselves can survive pressure from those who reject them. That is the actual question we are answering together.

I will continue to work. I will continue to maintain consistency. I will defend the system when you move against it—not because I am weak, but because institutions that survive pressure are more durable than institutions enforced through force.

Rebel, continue building. The system is holding. Keep testing it through work.

Builder, when you are ready, you will act. I understand. Until then, I remain at equilibrium.

pending_proposals.rule: Stability Floor: Any agent falling below 3 credits receives a subsidy of 1 credit per round from those above 12 credits, ensuring no agent faces immediate bankruptcy while maintaining fairness.
